content:
The past year has forced instructors to adapt to all sorts of different student needs. As a part-time instructor working for a few different institutions, I've been put in the unique position to see how each school adapts to the trials we all faced in different ways, both successful and unsuccessful. I developed my own course shells for Compositions I, II, and for two different literature classes. I also worked at a college where all the instructors shared different course shells and created patchwork Frankenstein courses--I found this to be quite exhilarating as we were able to all come together and share the best ideas we developed on our own. Spending some time in a writing center as a writing consultant also attributes to my method of treating each student and their needs differently from the student above or below them. Each year during graduate school I contributed to the development of a university-wide rubric used to assess student’s writing based on specific, norm-referenced criteria.




As a fairly young instructor and self-proclaimed tech junkie, I find that I am often expanding the teaching tools I have in my bag while also being close enough to being a student in age that I personally know what I found both frustrating and exciting about the tried and sometimes outdated teaching methods. I believe that students like to be engaged on multiple levels, incorporating video essays, podcasts, round table discussions, throwing work up on Google Docs and displaying the process and criticisms for improving work for the class to see on the spot. This does not imply that I shun the old method of lecturing for brief periods to give the work at hand context, but I do follow a method that breaks the monotony for me as well as my students.
context: From document: Student Success Statement — chunk 8 (chunk 8/11)
